title: Plan a Stage 4 Mathematics Exhibition
overview: Bring together everything from Stage 4 Mathematics to design a mathematics exhibition that teaches visitors the most important ideas from this stage.

project.drivingQuestion: How can we use the mathematics from Stage 4 to plan a clear, accurate and welcoming exhibition for our school or community?
project.finalProduct: Create an exhibition plan with labelled displays, a schedule, a budget, a visitor survey with a data display, and a short explanation of your mathematical decisions.

stages.title: 1. Term 1 foundations display
stages.prompt: Choose the two most important ideas from Term 1 (Units 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6). Build a display that teaches each idea with a model, an example and a check question.
stages.evidence: Two labelled displays with models, examples and check questions

stages.title: 2. Term 2 applications corner
stages.prompt: Design an activity corner where visitors use Term 2 skills (Units 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12) to solve one real problem, showing every step of the method.
stages.evidence: A worked real problem with each method step shown

stages.title: 3. Term 3 connections wall
stages.prompt: Create a connections wall that links Term 3 ideas (Units 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18) to earlier units, with at least three labelled connections.
stages.evidence: A connections wall with three labelled links between units

stages.title: 4. Present and explain
stages.prompt: Present your exhibition plan. Explain at least three mathematical choices, check that your answers are sensible and identify one improvement you would make.
stages.evidence: Spoken, written or recorded mathematical explanation

evidenceChecklist: A worked real problem with method steps
A connections wall with three links
A schedule and budget for the exhibition
A survey with organised data display

rubric.secure: Calculations, measures, shapes, data and models are accurate and checked.
The plan connects ideas from several Stage 4 units for a clear purpose.
Labels, diagrams, tables or charts make the mathematics visible.
Decisions are explained using appropriate mathematical words and evidence.

questions.question: Round 2,707 to the nearest 100.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2707 rounds to 2,700 — look at the tens digit.

questions.question: Round 6,008 to the nearest 10.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 6008 rounds to 6,010 — look at the ones digit.

questions.question: How many minutes are there in 6 hours?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: A lesson starts at 2 o'clock and lasts 2 hours. At what hour does it end?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Work out 487 + 299 in your head.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
questions.explanation: Round 299 up to 300 (that is 1 too many). 487 + 300 = 787. Give back the extra 1: 787 − 1 = 786.
questions.unitTitle: Addition and Subtraction of Whole Numbers

questions.question: Calculate 2374 + 1968 using the column method.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 4 + 8 = 12, write 2 carry 1. Tens: 1 + 7 + 6 = 14, write 4 carry 1. Hundreds: 1 + 3 + 9 = 13, write 3 carry 1. Thousands: 1 + 2 + 1 = 4. The answer is 4342.
questions.unitTitle: Addition and Subtraction of Whole Numbers

questions.question: Here is some data: 11, 3, 18, 2, 7. What is the range?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Here is some data: 19, 9, 10, 17, 14. What is the largest value?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Work out 8 × 7.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
questions.explanation: If you do not recall it instantly, use known facts. 5 × 7 = 35 and 3 × 7 = 21, and 5 + 3 = 8, so 8 × 7 = 35 + 21 = 56. You can check with commutativity: 7 × 8 is the same, and 7 × 8 = 56. Both agree.

questions.question: Work out 7 × 28.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
questions.explanation: Partition 28 into 20 + 8. Then 7 × 20 = 140 and 7 × 8 = 56. Add them: 140 + 56 = 196. This is the distributive property doing the hard work for you.

questions.question: How many degrees are there in a full turn?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: How many sides does a hexagon have?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Which fraction is larger: 1/2 or 1/4?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Halves are bigger than quarters, so 1/2 is larger than 1/4.

questions.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 1/2?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.question: How many sides does a hexagon have?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: How many degrees are there in a right angle?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Estimate 472 + 128 by rounding each number to the nearest 100.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 9 methods.
questions.explanation: 472 rounds to 500 (the tens digit 7 rounds up). 128 rounds to 100 (the tens digit 2 rounds down). Add the tidy numbers: 500 + 100 = 600. (The exact answer is 600 as well — a lucky, tidy result here.)

questions.question: Work out 56 ÷ 8, then check your answer.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 9 methods.
questions.explanation: Ask, what times 8 makes 56? From the 8 times table, 7 × 8 = 56, so 56 ÷ 8 = 7. Check with the inverse: 7 × 8 = 56. It matches, so the answer is correct.

questions.question: Here is some data: 5, 10, 16, 6, 7, 5. Which value appears most often (the mode)?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 5 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

questions.question: A tally shows these counts: 8, 15, 13, 5, 17. What is the total?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Which fraction is larger: 1/2 or 1/4?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Halves are bigger than quarters, so 1/2 is larger than 1/4.

questions.question: What is 1/5 of 85?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: How many edges does a cube have?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: How many vertices does a cube have?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Calculate 734 + 567.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 13 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 4 + 7 = 11, write 1 carry 1. Tens: 3 + 6 + 1 = 10, write 0 carry 1. Hundreds: 7 + 5 + 1 = 13, write 13. The answer is 1,301. Estimate check: 700 + 600 = 1,300, which is very close, so 1,301 is sensible.

questions.question: Calculate 904 − 657.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 13 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 4 − 7 is not possible, so regroup. There are no tens to take from, so first regroup a hundred into tens (9 hundreds become 8 hundreds and 10 tens), then regroup one of those tens into ones (10 tens become 9 tens and 14 ones). Now…

questions.question: How many millilitres are in 4 litres?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: A rectangle is 15 cm wide and 14 cm tall. What is its perimeter in centimetres?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Which number is greater: 5,936 or 914?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: What number comes just after 4,006?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Here is some data: 5, 16, 7, 10, 4. What is the largest value?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: A tally shows these counts: 9, 20, 10, 4, 15. What is the total?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Calculate 78 × 6.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 17 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate first: 80 × 6 = 480. Ones: 8 × 6 = 48, write 8, carry 4. Tens: 7 × 6 = 42, add the carried 4 to get 46, write 46. Answer: 468, close to the estimate of 480.

questions.question: Calculate 462 × 7.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 17 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate: 500 × 7 = 3,500. Ones: 2 × 7 = 14, write 4, carry 1. Tens: 6 × 7 = 42, add 1 to get 43, write 3, carry 4. Hundreds: 4 × 7 = 28, add 4 to get 32, write 32. Answer: 3,234, near the estimate.

questions.question: How many sides does a octagon have?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: How many edges does a cube have?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
